---
title: "Minimal Repro: Multi-line LaTeX macro + Markdown footnote"
subtitle: "Does pandoc expand user-defined macros during PDF builds?"
date: today
format:
  html: default
  pdf:
    keep-tex: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{makeidx}
        \makeindex
---

## Context

In a Quarto book (HTML + PDF), every chapter includes `latex/latex-commands.qmd`
via `{{< include >}}`. That file defines fallback index macros for HTML contexts,
written in the standard LaTeX multi-line style (with `%` at line-ends to suppress
whitespace in the macro expansion):

```latex
\providecommand{\ixd}[1]{%
  \index{#1@\texttt{#1} data}%
  \index{datasets!#1@\texttt{#1}}%
}
```

When XeLaTeX expands `\ixd{Prestige}`, the `%` acts as a TeX comment
(discards the trailing newline), so the expansion is
`\index{...}\index{...}` with no literal `%`. This is correct.

**The observed error:** The generated `index.tex` (Quarto's internal file
compiled by xelatex) contained the macro already *expanded*, with literal
`%` characters from the definition body:

```latex
\texttt{Prestige}%
  \index{Prestige@\texttt{Prestige} data}%
  \index{datasets!Prestige@\texttt{Prestige}}%.\footnote{...}
```

The `%` before `.\footnote{` caused XeLaTeX to never open the footnote,
producing `! Extra }, or forgotten \endgroup`.

**The question these tests answer:** Does pandoc expand user-defined LaTeX
macros — inserting literal `%` from multi-line definition bodies — during
its markdown-to-LaTeX conversion pass?

No filter is used in this repro. Results are in @sec-results.

---

## Test 1: Static `{=latex}` block + raw inline + footnote {#sec-test1}

A `{=latex}` raw block defines a multi-line macro; a `{=tex}` raw inline
uses it; a Markdown footnote follows immediately.

```{=latex}
\providecommand{\myone}[1]{%
  \textit{T1: #1}%
}
```

Static inline then footnote:
`\myone{alpha}`{=tex}.[^t1]

[^t1]: Test 1 footnote. If the PDF build fails here with `Extra }`, pandoc
  expanded `\myone` during conversion (inserting literal `%`).

---

## Test 2: Definition from `{{< include >}}` {#sec-test2}

In the book, `\providecommand` definitions come from an included file
(`latex/latex-commands.qmd`), not directly in the chapter. Quarto resolves
`{{< include >}}` before the pandoc pass. The resolved content is also
embedded in the freeze cache (`tex.json`); a stale cache can preserve
old multi-line definitions even after the source file is corrected — because
Quarto's freeze invalidation does not track `{{< include >}}` dependencies.

{{< include quarto-pandoc-test-macros.qmd >}}

`\mytwo{beta}`{=tex}.[^t2]

[^t2]: Test 2 footnote. If this fails but Test 1 passes, the issue is
  specific to included-file definitions (or stale freeze cache content).

---

## Test 3: Knitr-emitted raw inline + footnote {#sec-test3}

The book uses inline R expressions that emit raw LaTeX macros for PDF output
(`dataset("Prestige")` → `\texttt{Prestige}\ixd{Prestige}`). This test
simulates that via a knitr code chunk.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
  cat("\\mythree{gamma}")
} else {
  cat("*gamma*")
}
```
.[^t3]

[^t3]: Test 3 footnote. If this fails but Tests 1–2 pass, the issue is
  specific to knitr-emitted raw inline content (and how it is stored and
  replayed from the freeze cache). (`\mythree` is defined via `{{< include >}}`
  in Test 2.)

---

## Results {#sec-results}

Built with Quarto 1.8.25 on Windows 11, no Lua filters. Inspecting the
generated `.tex` file:

**Tests 1 and 2 — macros pass through unexpanded (PASS):**

```latex
\providecommand{\myone}[1]{%
  \textit{T1: #1}%
}
...
Static inline then footnote: \myone{alpha}.\footnote{Test 1 footnote...}

\providecommand{\mytwo}[1]{%   (from {{< include >}})
  \textit{T2: #1}%
}
...
\mytwo{beta}.\footnote{Test 2 footnote...}
```

Both `\myone{alpha}.\footnote{...}` and `\mytwo{beta}.\footnote{...}`
appear on a single line, unexpanded. XeLaTeX handles the expansion natively;
the `%` in the definition body acts as a TeX comment — no literal `%` in the
result, no `Extra }`. The PDF compiled successfully.

**Test 3 — knitr output creates a paragraph break (different issue):**

```latex
\mythree{gamma}

.\footnote{Test 3 footnote...}
```

A knitr code chunk adds `\n\n` after its output, splitting the macro call and
the `.[^t3]` footnote into separate paragraphs. The macro itself is still
unexpanded, but the `.` and `\footnote{}` end up in a new paragraph rather
than immediately following the inline. This is a separate formatting issue,
not the `Extra }` error.

**Conclusion:** Pandoc does **not** expand multi-line user-defined LaTeX macros
during PDF builds. Raw `{=latex}` blocks and `{=tex}` inlines pass through
to XeLaTeX unchanged. The `%` in multi-line `\providecommand` bodies is inert
in the pandoc output; XeLaTeX interprets it correctly as a comment.

The `Extra }` error in the book was caused by the `parse-latex` Quarto
extension, which calls `pandoc.read(raw.text, 'latex')` on every raw LaTeX
block. When `parse-latex` was listed in the **global** `filters:` (not
`format: html: filters:`), it ran for both HTML and PDF. The
`pandoc.read()` call registered multi-line `\providecommand` definitions in
pandoc's macro table, then expanded subsequent macro usages — inserting the
literal `%` characters from the definition bodies into the pandoc output,
where they were no longer TeX comments. Moving `parse-latex` to
`format: html: filters:` and clearing stale freeze caches resolved the error.

---

## How to run

```bash
# From the project root:
quarto render issues/quarto-pandoc-test.qmd --to pdf
quarto render issues/quarto-pandoc-test.qmd --to html
```

Inspect `issues/quarto-pandoc-test.tex` to verify whether macros are
expanded or passed through to XeLaTeX.
